title: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Understanding SARS-CoV-2 Transmission Dynamics
items.content: The basic reproduction number ($R_0$) of SARS-CoV-2 is estimated to peak around 6, indicating a higher transmission potential compared to seasonal influenza. This high $R_0$ value underscores the virus's ability to spread rapidly in densely populated settings and among individuals with close contact.
keyPoints: Basic reproduction number ($R_0$) of SARS-CoV-2 peaks around 6.
$R_0$ for seasonal influenza is typically 1–2.
Densely populated settings facilitate high transmission efficiency.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Role of Superspreaders in SARS-CoV-2 Transmission
items.content: Certain individuals, known as 'superspreaders,' have been identified as contributing to extraordinarily high transmission events. This highlights the importance of identifying and managing such cases to control outbreaks.
keyPoints: Superspreaders contribute to high transmission events.
Identifying superspreaders is crucial for outbreak management.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Viral Variants on Public Health Measures
items.content: The rapid evolution of SARS-CoV-2 variants affects transmission, disease severity, and the effectiveness of vaccines and therapies. The WHO's classification system for variants under monitoring (VUMs), variants of interest (VOIs), and variants of concern (VOCs) helps track these changes.
keyPoints: Variants affect transmission, severity, and vaccine efficacy.
WHO classifies variants as VUMs, VOIs, and VOCs.
CoViNet launched in 2024 for early detection and tracking.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Risk Factors for Severe COVID-19
items.content: Advanced age, male sex, and certain comorbidities significantly increase the risk of severe illness from COVID-19. Understanding these factors is crucial for identifying high-risk patients who may require more intensive monitoring or early intervention.
keyPoints: More than 95% of COVID-19 deaths occur in persons older than 45 years, and >80% of deaths occur in those older than 65 years.
Male sex is associated with higher risk of severe disease (odds ratio, ~1.8).
Most individuals who die from COVID-19 have preexisting comorbidities.
Elevated BMI increases the risk of severe illness: overweight (BMI >25 but <30), obesity (BMI ≥30 but <40), and severe obesity (BMI ≥40).
Substance use disorders and current or former smoking increase risk.
Pregnant women are more likely to suffer from severe illness.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Comorbidities Increasing Risk of Severe COVID-19
items.content: Certain medical conditions significantly elevate the risk for severe COVID-19. Recognizing these comorbidities can guide clinical decision-making and prioritization of care.
keyPoints: Chronic lung diseases (e.g., COPD, asthma), cancer or cancer treatments, immunodeficiency, hemoglobin disorders, cerebrovascular disease, cognitive impairment, heart conditions, obstructive sleep apnea, chronic inflammatory/autoimmune/rheumatic diseases, diabetes mellitus, and chronic liver disease are key risk factors.
Genetic conditions like Down syndrome also increase the risk of severe illness.

MIS-C in children is associated with COVID-19, affecting multiple organ systems and showing elevated inflammatory markers. The highest-risk group for MIS-C in the U.S. includes Black and Latino children aged 3–12 years.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Nonpharmaceutical Interventions (NPIs) in Early COVID-19 Prevention
items.content: In the early stages of the COVID-19 epidemic, NPIs such as social distancing, isolation, quarantine, travel limitations, and remote work were crucial due to limited pharmaceutical options. These measures aimed to reduce transmission by minimizing contact between individuals.
keyPoints: Social distancing involves staying at least 6 ft away from others in public settings.
Quarantine duration is typically 14 days after potential exposure.
Screening for symptoms like fever, cough, dyspnea, sore throat, myalgias, and anosmia/hyposmia before entering healthcare settings.
Universal masking was mandated in many healthcare settings during the epidemic.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Clinical Course of COVID-19: Spectrum from Asymptomatic to Critical Disease
items.content: COVID-19 presents a wide range of clinical manifestations, from asymptomatic cases to severe and critical conditions requiring intensive care. Understanding this spectrum is vital for appropriate patient management and resource allocation.
keyPoints: A substantial proportion (possibly a third) of infected individuals are asymptomatic but can transmit the virus.
Most symptomatic infections result in mild disease without pneumonia.
Severe disease, involving pneumonia and symptoms like dyspnea or hypoxia with oxygen saturation ≤ 94%, often requires hospitalization.
Critical disease may involve respiratory failure, multiorgan failure, or shock, necessitating intensive care.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Timing and Types of COVID-19 Diagnostic Tests
items.content: COVID-19 testing is recommended during periods of respiratory symptoms or 5 days after exposure without active symptoms. There are two major types of tests: antigen tests, which detect proteins and are often used as rapid screening tools, and molecular tests (NAATs), such as RT-PCR, which detect genetic material with higher accuracy.
keyPoints: Testing recommended during respiratory symptoms or 5 days after exposure without symptoms.
Antigen tests: Detect proteins, less accurate than molecular tests, often used for rapid screening.
Molecular tests (NAATs): Use RT-PCR to detect viral RNA with high accuracy; not rapid and require professional settings.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.title: Imaging and Laboratory Findings in COVID-19 Diagnosis
items.content: Chest radiographs may show abnormalities like consolidation and ground-glass opacities, but can also be normal despite respiratory issues. Chest CT is more sensitive than radiographs and shows specific features for COVID-19, though it's primarily used for managing respiratory disease rather than initial diagnosis.
keyPoints: Chest radiographs: May show bilateral consolidation and ground-glass opacities; may be normal with respiratory compromise.
Chest CT: More sensitive than radiographs, shows typical features like ground-glass opacifications; used for managing respiratory disease.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.title: Long COVID: Chronic Condition with Neuropsychiatric and Metabolic Symptoms
items.content: Long COVID is a chronic condition that persists for at least 3 months following SARS-CoV-2 infection. It encompasses a wide array of symptoms, including neuropsychiatric disorders, pain syndromes, respiratory changes, and metabolic alterations.
keyPoints: Long COVID lasts for at least 3 months post-infection.
Symptoms include neuropsychiatric disorders, pain syndromes, respiratory issues, and metabolic changes.
Increased risk of suicidal ideation in those with long COVID.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Severe Complications of COVID-19: Multiorgan Involvement and High Mortality Risk
items.content: Severe COVID-19 can lead to multiple complications, including acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), disseminated intravascular coagulation, thromboembolic events, cardiac issues, acute kidney injury, encephalopathy, and neurologic deficits. An excessive inflammatory response is linked to severe outcomes.
keyPoints: Major complication: ARDS requiring oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation.
Common complications include venous thromboembolism (e.g., pulmonary embolism, deep vein thrombosis) and arterial thrombosis (e.g., acute stroke).
Cardiac issues may manifest as heart failure, myocardial injury, or arrhythmias.
Acute kidney injury can necessitate dialysis.
Encephalopathy and delirium in ICU settings reduce survival rates.
Excessive inflammatory response contributes to severe pulmonary pathology.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Anticoagulation in COVID-19 Management
items.content: Pharmacologic prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism is recommended for all hospitalized patients with COVID-19 due to the substantial risk of thromboembolic complications.
keyPoints: COVID-19 increases risk of thromboembolic events in hospitalized patients.
Prophylactic anticoagulation is advised for these patients.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.title: Recovery Timeline and Long-term Effects of COVID-19
items.content: Symptomatic infection from COVID-19 typically resolves in about 2 weeks (~80% of persons) following mild or moderate disease, while severe cases may take several months to recover. A subset experiences persistent symptoms such as fatigue, cognitive deficits, cough, dyspnea, or chest pain.
keyPoints: ~80% of individuals with mild/moderate COVID-19 recover in 2 weeks.
Severe cases may require recovery over several months.
Persistent post-COVID symptoms include fatigue, cognitive issues, and respiratory problems.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.title: Early Use of Antiviral Drugs in COVID-19 Management
items.content: Antiviral drugs, including small-molecule inhibitors and monoclonal antibodies, are most effective when administered early in the infection. They can potentially alter the clinical course by reducing the peak titer of the virus, which is likely correlated with disease severity.
keyPoints: Antivirals may reduce peak viral titer, correlating with reduced disease severity.
Most beneficial when used early in the infection.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.title: Role of Anti-inflammatory Medications in Later COVID-19 Disease Course
items.content: As COVID-19 progresses, anti-inflammatory medications become more beneficial. This is because the pathogenesis shifts from direct viral effects to tissue inflammation and systemic inflammatory responses.
keyPoints: Anti-inflammatories are more effective later in disease progression.
Pathogenesis driven by inflammation rather than direct viral cytopathic effect.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.title: Criteria for Antiviral Use in Outpatient COVID-19 Management
items.content: Antiviral drugs should be considered for symptomatic patients with specific risk factors, such as older age, lack of vaccination, multiple comorbidities, or immunocompromised status. The decision to use antivirals must balance potential benefits against risks like adverse effects and drug interactions.
keyPoints: Antiviral therapy is not recommended for asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection.
Beneficial candidates include older adults (>50 years), those unvaccinated, with multiple comorbidities, or immunocompromised individuals.
Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir should be administered within 5 days of symptom onset; dose adjustments are necessary for reduced renal function.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.title: Management and Monitoring During Antiviral Treatment for COVID-19
items.content: Patients receiving antiviral treatment should be monitored closely for any worsening symptoms, particularly mental status changes or respiratory issues. They should also adhere to self-isolation guidelines as per CDC recommendations.
keyPoints: Counsel patients on monitoring for worsening or new-onset symptoms like confusion or dyspnea.
Advise adherence to CDC-recommended isolation periods post-treatment.
Reevaluation is necessary if there's a rebound of viral shedding after initial improvement.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.title: Alternative Antiviral Options and Their Considerations in Outpatient COVID-19 Treatment
items.content: When nirmatrelvir-ritonavir is unavailable or unsuitable, alternatives like remdesivir and molnupiravir can be considered. Remdesivir requires IV administration over three days, while molnupiravir has a black box warning due to its mutagenic properties.
keyPoints: Remdesivir is administered via three IV doses over 3 days.
Molnupiravir carries a black box warning for use in patients with pregnancy potential.
Both alternatives are more challenging to administer compared to nirmatrelvir-ritonavir.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.title: Criteria for Hospitalization of COVID-19 Patients
items.content: Hospitalization is recommended for COVID-19 patients exhibiting severe symptoms such as moderate to severe dyspnea, cyanosis, change in mental status or chest pain, reduced urine output or anuria, and oxygen saturation ≤94%. The NIH guidelines suggest hospitalization for patients with specific clinical indicators including oxygen saturation <94% on room air, respiratory rate >30 breaths/min, PaO₂/FiO₂ <300 mmHg, or lung infiltrates occupying >50% of lung fields.
keyPoints: Hospitalize if symptoms include moderate to severe dyspnea, cyanosis, change in mental status, chest pain, reduced urine output/anuria, or oxygen saturation ≤94%
NIH recommends hospitalization for: oxygen saturation <94% on room air, respiratory rate >30 breaths/min, PaO₂/FiO₂ <300 mmHg, lung infiltrates >50% of lung fields
Decision to hospitalize varies by region and resource availability
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.title: Management Strategies for Hospitalized COVID-19 Patients
items.content: Upon admission, assess comorbidities or immunocompromising factors and critical organ dysfunction. Control fever with acetaminophen; avoid NSAIDs unless necessary at lower doses. Continue chronic medications like aspirin (unless bleeding risk), statins, ACE inhibitors, or ARBs. Administer therapeutic-dose anticoagulation to prevent venous thromboembolism. Supportive care is primary for patients without severe disease risks, while remdesivir and low-flow oxygen are used for those with mild symptoms. Low-dose dexamethasone is added for its anti-inflammatory effects. For escalating oxygen needs or elevated inflammatory markers within the first 4 days, consider baricitinib or tocilizumab. High-dose glucocorticoids require monitoring for hyperglycemia and co-infection risks.
keyPoints: Assess comorbidities and organ dysfunction upon admission
Control fever with acetaminophen; avoid NSAIDs unless necessary at lower doses
Continue chronic medications like aspirin, statins, ACE inhibitors, or ARBs
Administer therapeutic-dose anticoagulation to prevent venous thromboembolism
Supportive care for patients without severe disease risks
Use remdesivir and low-flow oxygen for mild symptoms; add low-dose dexamethasone for anti-inflammatory effects
Consider baricitinib or tocilizumab if oxygen needs escalate within 4 days with elevated inflammatory markers
Monitor high-dose glucocorticoids for hyperglycemia and co-infection risks
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.title: Effectiveness of Antibody-Based Therapies in COVID-19
items.content: Antibody-based therapies, including convalescent plasma and human monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), have been explored for treating COVID-19. Convalescent plasma has a moderately low overall composite titer of SARS-CoV-2-neutralizing antibodies post-primary infection, limiting its effectiveness. Human mAbs are recombinant proteins developed from genes encoding an antibody from a convalescent individual's B cell. These therapies were initially granted Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) for high-risk outpatients with mild to moderate COVID-19 but have been revoked due to reduced efficacy against new variants.
keyPoints: Convalescent plasma has moderately low SARS-CoV-2-neutralizing antibody titers post-primary infection, limiting effectiveness.
Human mAbs are recombinant proteins derived from convalescent individuals' B cells.
EUA for human mAbs was revoked due to reduced efficacy against new variants.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Management of COVID-19 Complications
items.content: Severe and complicated COVID-19 affects multiple organ systems, with complications arising from hyperinflammatory and hypercoagulable states. Common issues include acute respiratory distress syndrome, viral sepsis, cardiac injury, arrhythmias, thromboembolic events, kidney injury, cytokine storm, oxidative stress, and shock. Bacterial superinfection is possible but uncertain in incidence. Empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy is not recommended without other indications, though some experts use it for patients with moderate or severe hypoxemia. Anticoagulation management during COVID-19-associated thromboembolic events requires expert consultation.
keyPoints: COVID-19 complications include respiratory and cardiovascular issues, among others, due to hyperinflammatory and hypercoagulable states.
Bacterial superinfection incidence is uncertain; empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobials are not generally recommended without other indications.
Anticoagulation management for COVID-19-associated thromboembolic events requires expert consultation.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.title: Mechanism and Duration of Long-Acting Antibodies in COVID-19 Prevention
items.content: Long-acting antibodies (LAABs) such as tixagevimab and cilgavimab were engineered to have extended serum half-lives by modifying the Fc portion for enhanced binding to the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn). This modification allows these antibodies to provide longer-term protection against COVID-19, particularly in individuals who cannot be vaccinated effectively.
keyPoints: IgG antibodies typically have a serum half-life of about 21 days.
Tixagevimab and cilgavimab were authorized for emergency use as LAABs to protect immunocompromised individuals.
Modification in the Fc portion alters kinetics, enhancing retention via FcRn binding.
FDA terminated EUA for tixagevimab/cilgavimab in January 2023 due to escape variants.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.title: Criteria and Administration of Pemivibart for COVID-19 Prophylaxis
items.content: Pemivibart, another LAAB, was granted EUA in 2024 for pre-exposure prophylaxis in adults and adolescents aged ≥12 years weighing ≥40 kg with moderate to severe immune compromise. It is administered intravenously due to its large dose (4.5 g) and is used when the national frequency of variants with reduced susceptibility is ≤90%.
keyPoints: Pemivibart granted EUA in 2024 for pre-exposure prophylaxis.
Indicated for individuals ≥12 years, weighing ≥40 kg with moderate to severe immune compromise.
IV administration required due to a large dose of 4.5 g.
Use is limited when variant susceptibility reduction is ≤90%.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.title: Efficacy of mRNA Vaccines in Preventing Symptomatic COVID-19
items.content: The original mRNA vaccines (Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna) demonstrated a high efficacy rate of 95% for preventing symptomatic COVID-19 during their phase 3 clinical trials. This rapid development was possible due to prior research on related coronaviruses.
keyPoints: mRNA vaccines showed 95% efficacy in preventing symptomatic COVID-19
Rapid vaccine development leveraged previous coronavirus research
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.title: Need for Vaccine Updates Due to Variant Emergence
items.content: The emergence of antigenic variant viruses has necessitated the updating of mRNA vaccines. Since their introduction, mRNAs encoding spike protein antigens have been replaced three times to target different Omicron variants, with further updates in 2023 and 2024 for new sublineages.
keyPoints: mRNA vaccines updated multiple times since original deployment
Updates targeted emerging Omicron variants including BA.4, BA.5, XBB, and KP.2
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.title: Effectiveness of the Protein Vaccine Against Severe Disease
items.content: The protein vaccine (Novavax) demonstrated 100% effectiveness against moderate and severe disease in phase 3 trials. It uses a recombinant S protein expressed in insect cells, formulated with an adjuvant to enhance immunogenicity.
keyPoints: Protein vaccine showed 100% effectiveness against moderate and severe COVID-19
Uses recombinant S protein with Matrix-M adjuvant
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following proteins is NOT one of the structural proteins encoded by SARS-CoV-2?
items.explanation: The structural proteins encoded by SARS-CoV-2 are the spike (S), envelope (E), membrane (M), and nucleocapsid (N) proteins. There is no capsid protein.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which receptor does the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein bind to facilitate entry into human cells?
items.explanation: The SARS-CoV-2 spike protein binds to the human angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (hACE2) receptor.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: What is the estimated peak basic reproduction number ($ R_{0} $) for SARS-CoV-2?
items.explanation: The basic reproduction number (R0) of SARS-CoV-2 has been estimated to peak around 6.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following settings is NOT mentioned as facilitating high transmission efficiency for SARS-CoV-2?
items.explanation: Transmission in outdoor settings is less common, unlike densely populated settings such as prisons, cruise ships, and nursing homes.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which group has the highest risk of severe illness from COVID-19?
items.explanation: More than 95% of COVID-19 deaths occur in persons older than 45 years.

items.question: Which condition is NOT listed as a risk factor for severe COVID-19?
items.explanation: Chronic kidney disease is not listed among the conditions that especially increase risk.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which demographic group has a higher odds ratio for severe COVID-19?
items.explanation: Male sex is associated with a higher risk of severe disease (odds ratio, ~1.8).
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which nonpharmaceutical intervention involves staying at least 6 feet away from others in public to avoid infection?
items.explanation: Social distancing is defined as maintaining a distance of at least 6 feet from other persons in public.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is the recommended duration for quarantine after potential exposure to COVID-19?
items.explanation: Quarantine typically involves staying at home for 14 days following potential exposure.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common clinical sign or symptom screened for in patients before entering health care settings during an epidemic?
items.explanation: Headache is not listed as a common clinical sign or symptom screened for in the passage.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which type of COVID-19 diagnostic test is typically performed using reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR)?
items.explanation: Molecular tests, specifically nucleic acid amplification tests like RT-PCR, are used to detect the genetic material of SARS-CoV-2.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.question: Which sample type is most commonly used for COVID-19 testing?
items.explanation: Nasopharyngeal swabs are the most commonly used sample type for COVID-19 testing.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature typically interpreted on chest CT scans for COVID-19?
items.explanation: Cavitary lesions are not typically interpreted as a feature of COVID-19 on chest CT scans.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.question: Which of the following is a common symptom that may manifest in patients with COVID-19?
items.explanation: Sudden onset of dysgeusia (loss of taste) occurs in a substantial number of COVID-19 cases.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is a major complication in patients with severe COVID-19?
items.explanation: The major complication in severe disease is acute respiratory distress syndrome requiring oxygen therapy and mechanical ventilation.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which of the following complications can occur due to COVID-19?
items.explanation: Disseminated intravascular coagulation is a complication in severe COVID-19 disease.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which antipyretic agent is preferred over NSAIDs for patients with COVID-19 due to concerns about worse outcomes?
items.explanation: Acetaminophen is preferred over NSAIDs as antipyretic agents in COVID-19 due to concerns about worse outcomes with NSAID use.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.question: What prophylactic measure is recommended for all hospitalized patients with COVID-19 to prevent thromboembolic complications?
items.explanation: Pharmacologic prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism is recommended for all hospitalized patients with COVID-19 to prevent thromboembolic complications.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.question: In the context of COVID-19, why might immunosuppressed individuals require special consideration regarding their medication regimen?
options: They are at lower risk of severe illness
Their medications may need to be discontinued immediately
Immunomodulatory agents should always be stopped
They are at higher risk of severe illness or death
items.explanation: Immunosuppressed individuals are at higher risk of severe illness or death, requiring careful consideration regarding the continuation of immunomodulatory agents.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which category of treatments for COVID-19 has the potential to alter the clinical course early in infection?
options: Antiviral drugs (including small-molecule inhibitors and polyclonal or monoclonal antibodies)
items.explanation: Antiviral drugs can reduce the peak titer of virus early in infection, potentially altering the clinical course.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.question: What is a primary reason for using anti-inflammatory medications later in the COVID-19 disease course?
options: To decrease tissue inflammation and systemic inflammatory responses
items.explanation: Later in the clinical course, anti-inflammatory medications are beneficial due to increased tissue inflammation and systemic inflammatory responses.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.question: Under what condition can medical countermeasures be authorized for use before full FDA approval?
options: When there is a public health emergency declared by the WHO
When they receive unanimous support from all international health organizations
When an EUA is appropriate for a public health emergency as declared by the secretary of the Department of Health and Human Services
When they have completed Phase III clinical trials
items.explanation: The FDA can authorize medical countermeasures under EUA when there's a public health emergency declared by the HHS Secretary.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.question: Which group is most likely to benefit from specific antiviral treatment for COVID-19?
options: Symptomatic patients who are older, unvaccinated, and have multiple medical comorbidities or immunocompromised conditions.
All adults regardless of age, vaccination status, or comorbidities.
Patients with mild COVID-19 symptoms who do not require hospitalization.
items.explanation: Symptomatic patients who are older, unvaccinated, and have multiple medical comorbidities or immunocompromised conditions are most likely to benefit from specific antiviral treatment.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: When should nirmatrelvir-ritonavir be administered for outpatient COVID-19 treatment?
options: As soon as possible and within 5 days after symptom onset.
Only after hospitalization is considered necessary.
After consulting with a pharmacist about drug interactions.
items.explanation: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir should be administered as soon as possible and within 5 days after symptom onset for outpatient COVID-19 treatment.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is a potential risk of using COVID-19-specific antiviral medications?
options: They are ineffective against all SARS-CoV-2 variants.
They may cause adverse effects or drug-drug interactions.
They can only be administered intravenously.
They eliminate the need for self-isolation.
items.explanation: COVID-19-specific antiviral medications have the potential to cause adverse effects or drug-drug interactions.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for individuals with asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection?
items.explanation: COVID-19-specific therapy is not recommended for individuals with asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which of the following criteria is NOT recommended by the NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines Panel for hospitalization?
options: Oxygen saturation of <94% on room air
Lung infiltrates occupying >75% of lung fields
items.explanation: The NIH guidelines recommend hospitalization for lung infiltrates on chest radiograph occupying >50%, not >75%.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which medication is suggested to be continued in hospitalized COVID-19 patients unless there's a clear bleeding risk?
items.explanation: Aspirin should generally be continued unless there is a clear bleeding risk.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: What treatment is recommended for hospitalized COVID-19 patients with low-flow supplemental oxygen?
items.explanation: Patients receiving low-flow supplemental oxygen are treated with remdesivir for antiviral effect and low-dose dexamethasone for anti-inflammatory treatment.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which of the following statements about convalescent plasma for SARS-CoV-2 is correct?
options: It has a high and consistent titer of neutralizing antibodies.
It typically shows low overall composite titers, limiting its effectiveness.
It is derived from individuals currently infected with COVID-19.
It is the most effective treatment for severe COVID-19.
items.explanation: Convalescent plasma has a moderately low titer of SARS-CoV-2-neutralizing antibodies, limiting its effectiveness and reproducibility.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the primary reason for revoking EUAs for human monoclonal antibodies in COVID-19 treatment?
options: They were found to cause severe side effects.
New variants have escaped their activity.
They are ineffective against all strains of SARS-CoV-2.
items.explanation: EUAs for monoclonal antibodies were revoked because new variants have escaped their activity.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.question: Which of the following is a common complication in severe COVID-19?
options: Acute respiratory distress syndrome with severe hypoxia.
items.explanation: Acute respiratory distress syndrome with severe hypoxia is a common complication in severe COVID-19.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.question: What is the average serum half-life of IgG antibodies?
items.explanation: IgG antibodies typically have an average serum half-life of about 21 days.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.question: Which combination drug received emergency use authorization for long-term prevention of COVID-19?
items.explanation: The two-antibody combination drug tixagevimab co-packaged with cilgavimab received emergency use authorization.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.question: What was the reason for terminating the EUA of the first LAAB in January 2023?
items.explanation: The FDA terminated the EUA due to the widespread emergence of escape variants in circulation.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.question: Which COVID-19 vaccines are based on mRNA technology?
items.explanation: The mRNA vaccines authorized in the United States are from Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.question: What was the reason for withdrawing the EUA of a viral vector vaccine in June 2023?
options: Association with thrombosis with thrombocytopenia syndrome (TTS)
items.explanation: The EUA was withdrawn due to the association with thrombosis with thrombocytopenia syndrome (TTS).
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.question: Which age group is eligible for the subunit protein vaccine?
items.explanation: The subunit protein vaccine is available for anyone 12 years and older.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.question: What was the primary goal of COVID-19 vaccination?
options: Reducing critical illness or death from COVID-19
items.explanation: The principal goal of vaccination is preventing severe COVID-19 by reducing critical illness or death.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.statement: The WHO declared the end of the global COVID-19 emergency on May 5, 2023.
items.explanation: On May 5, 2023, the WHO declared an end to the global COVID-19 emergency.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.statement: Airborne transmission of SARS-CoV-2 over long distances is common.
items.explanation: Airborne transmission by small particles from person-to-person may occur, but airborne transmission over long distances is unlikely.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Most COVID-19 deaths occur in individuals younger than 45 years.
items.explanation: More than 95% of COVID-19 deaths occur in persons older than 45 years.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Substance use disorders increase the risk for severe COVID-19.
items.explanation: Substance use, such as alcohol, opioid, or cocaine use disorder, increases risk.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Most mask studies have been randomized controlled trials.
items.explanation: The passage states that most mask studies have been observational.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: A substantial proportion of COVID-19 patients are asymptomatic and can transmit the virus.
items.explanation: The passage notes that a substantial proportion of infected individuals are asymptomatic but can still transmit the virus.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Antigen tests for COVID-19 are more accurate than molecular tests, especially in asymptomatic individuals.
items.explanation: Antigen tests are less accurate than molecular tests, particularly when testing exposed persons without symptoms.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.statement: Chest CT scans should be used as a primary diagnostic tool for COVID-19.
items.explanation: CT scans are more sensitive than radiographs but should primarily be used for managing respiratory disease, not as the main diagnostic tool.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.statement: Long COVID can present with neuropsychiatric disorders and pain syndromes.
items.explanation: Long COVID is a chronic condition that includes varied symptoms such as neuropsychiatric disorders.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: COVID-19 complications are limited to respiratory issues.
items.explanation: Complications include thromboembolic events, cardiac issues, acute kidney injury, and neurologic complications.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Bacterial infection is a common complication in COVID-19 patients.
items.explanation: Bacterial infection is an uncommon complication of COVID-19.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Most individuals with mild or moderate COVID-19 recover within 2 weeks.
items.explanation: Symptomatic infection typically resolves in ~80% of persons within 2 weeks, especially following mild or moderate disease.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.statement: All guidelines from expert groups for COVID-19 treatments are harmonized.
items.explanation: Many but not all of the guidelines from such groups are harmonized.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.statement: The WHO ended Public Health Emergency of International Concern status for COVID on May 5, 2023.
items.explanation: The WHO did end the Public Health Emergency of International Concern status for COVID on May 5, 2023.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.statement: Antibiotics are recommended as routine treatment for COVID-19.
items.explanation: Antibiotics are not recommended for routine treatment of COVID-19, as bacterial complications are unusual.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Molnupiravir is approved without any restrictions.
items.explanation: Molnupiravir has a black box warning about its mutagenic properties, limiting its use in patients with pregnancy potential.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.statement: NSAIDs have been aligned with poor COVID-19 outcomes.
items.explanation: The use of NSAIDs has not been aligned with poor COVID-19 outcomes, but their use is generally avoided or limited.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Baricitinib may have direct antiviral effects by interfering with viral entry into cells.
items.explanation: Baricitinib may mediate some direct antiviral effects by interfering with viral entry.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Bacterial superinfection of COVID-19 has a well-determined incidence.
items.explanation: The incidence of bacterial superinfection in COVID-19 is uncertain.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy is recommended for all patients with moderate or severe hypoxemia due to COVID-19.
items.explanation: There are insufficient data to recommend empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy in the absence of another indication.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.statement: Pemivibart can be administered orally for pre-exposure prophylaxis.
items.explanation: Pemivibart requires IV administration due to the large dose required.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.statement: The use of LAABs is limited to individuals who cannot be effectively vaccinated.
items.explanation: LAABs are used as a vaccine surrogate for high-risk individuals who cannot mount an adequate immune response.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.statement: COVID vaccines were developed in record time due to prior research on related coronaviruses.
items.explanation: The rapid development of COVID-19 vaccines was facilitated by years of prior research on SARS-CoV-1 and MERS-CoV.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.statement: The mRNA vaccines have shown no waning in immunity over time.
items.explanation: Real-world studies indicated that the protection from mRNA vaccines waned over time.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.assertion: The WHO established a Technical Advisory Group on SARS-CoV-2 Virus Evolution.
items.reason: To follow and report variant evolution of the virus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the establishment of the group.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.subtopic: SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)
items.assertion: The WHO Coronavirus Network (CoViNet) was launched in 2024 to track variants.
items.reason: To facilitate early and accurate detection of coronaviruses and variant tracking.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for launching CoViNet.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 | CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19) | EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Advanced age is the principal risk factor for severe COVID-19 illness.
items.reason: More than 95% of COVID-19 deaths occur in persons older than 45 years.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: Most experts conclude that masking, use of personal protective equipment, and hand washing are highly appropriate in health care settings with infected patients.
items.reason: These measures may have some benefit in community settings as well.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTATIVE MEASURES | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Chest CT is more sensitive than chest radiographs in detecting COVID-19 abnormalities.
items.reason: CT scans can systematically interpret features such as ground-glass opacifications and mixed consolidation, which are indicative of COVID-19.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why CT is more sensitive.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · DIAGNOSIS OF COVID-19

items.assertion: An excessive proinflammatory host response to SARS-CoV-2 infection contributes directly to the severity of COVID-19.
items.reason: Manifestations typically mediated by autoantibodies have been reported in COVID-19 patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · CLINICAL COURSE | COMPLICATIONS

items.assertion: The time to recovery from COVID-19 is influenced by the severity of the disease.
items.reason: Recovery duration varies with disease severity, with severe cases often requiring several months for recovery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · GENERAL MEDICAL MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Antiviral drugs are most effective in altering the clinical course of COVID-19 early in infection.
items.reason: They may reduce the peak titer of virus, which is likely correlated with disease severity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why antiviral drugs are effective early in infection.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · SPECIFIC TREATMENTS

items.assertion: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir should be avoided in patients with severe renal dysfunction.
items.reason: The dose of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir should be adjusted for reduced renal function and should be avoided in patients with severe renal dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Virus-neutralizing antibodies have remained effective against all COVID-19 variants.
items.reason: New variants arise and reduce their effectiveness, often leading to withdrawal of authorization.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because virus-neutralizing antibodies do not remain effective against all COVID-19 variants; the reason is true.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Tocilizumab is used for its immunomodulatory effect in COVID-19 treatment.
items.reason: Tocilizumab blocks the effect of IL-6, which plays an important role in pathogenesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the use of tocilizumab.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · INPATIENT MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Anticoagulation is a straightforward treatment for COVID-19-associated thromboembolic events.
items.reason: COVID-19-associated thromboembolic events require expert consultation due to their complexity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because anticoagulation in the context of COVID-19-associated thromboembolic events is complex and requires expert consultation.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED THERAPIES | TREATMENT OF COMPLICATIONS

items.assertion: The FDA granted an EUA for pemivibart in 2024.
items.reason: Pemivibart is limited to use when the combined national frequency of variants with substantially reduced susceptibility is ≤90%.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the EUA was granted; it explains a condition for its use.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · ANTIBODY-BASED PREVENTION

items.assertion: The protein vaccine demonstrated 100% effectiveness against moderate and severe disease.
items.reason: This was shown in phase 3 trial results published in 2021.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS

items.assertion: The original mRNA vaccines from Pfizer and Moderna protected against all variants of SARS-CoV-2.
items.reason: These vaccines were updated to target different variants over time.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the original vaccines did not protect against all variants; they required updates.
items.reference: SARS CoV 2 and COVID 19 · PREVENTION BY IMMUNOPROPHYLAXIS
